title: Installing Fabric Under Decks Pavers and Sheds to Control Weeds
focusKeyword: fabric under decks pavers and sheds
author: Marcus Halvorsen

seo.metaTitle: Fabric Under Decks Pavers and Sheds | DOTDAY
seo.metaDescription: What fabric under decks pavers and sheds works best? 3.2oz for decks and weed control, 5oz for pavers and load. A practical install guide from DOTDAY.

social.excerpt: Installing fabric under decks, pavers, and sheds suppresses weeds, improves drainage, and extends the life of your structures. Here is how to do it right. #DOTDAY #landscapefabric #pavers #decks #weedbarrier

hero.excerpt: Installing fabric under decks, pavers, and sheds suppresses weeds, improves drainage, and extends the lifespan of your outdoor structures. Here is how and why it works.

image.alt: Fabric under decks pavers and sheds, installed with DOTDAY heavy-duty woven fabric
image.caption: For pavers and load-bearing bases, XBAR 5oz is the right weight; decks and sheds can use SHIELD.

quickAnswer.heading: What you'll learn
items: Why fabric under decks, pavers, and sheds controls weeds and aids drainage
Which weight to use: 3.2oz for decks and weed control, 5oz for pavers and load
A step-by-step install that beginners can follow
How overlap and anchoring keep weeds out of the seams
When to reach for SHIELD, XBAR, or TERRA

blocks.heading: Why use fabric under decks pavers and sheds
blocks.body: Tackling relentless weed growth quickly gets frustrating. Installing fabric under decks pavers and sheds creates a barrier that suppresses weeds while improving drainage and extending the lifespan of your structures. The main goal of this weed control fabric is to stop weeds infiltrating your paved and covered areas, but the benefits go further. A quality base fabric lets water pass through freely while preventing soil and debris from compacting under your structure, so a patio drains instead of pooling. It also acts as a separator against the soil movement that, over time, leads to settling and instability. Size your install with the [DOTDAY Fabric Calculator](https://www.thedotday.com/landscape-fabric-calculator).

blocks.heading: Which weight for which structure
rows: No load on the fabric, just weed suppression and airflow
Light duty separation and weed control under the floor
Carries the compacted base and traffic without tearing
Filters and flows water rather than bearing load

steps.title: Prepare the area
steps.body: Clear the zone for your deck, pavers, or shed. Remove vegetation, rocks, and debris, and aim for a level surface for stability.

steps.title: Lay the fabric
steps.body: Roll out the fabric across the cleared area for complete coverage. On larger areas, overlap sheets by at least 6 inches so weeds cannot find the seams.

steps.title: Anchor it
steps.body: Secure the fabric with stakes or pins every few feet so it stays put, especially in windy conditions.

steps.title: Excavate where needed
steps.body: For pavers or deck footings, dig to depth carefully without tearing the fabric where it needs to stay intact.

steps.title: Layer and finish
steps.body: For pavers, add crushed stone or gravel on top for drainage and a stable base. For sheds, follow the foundation guidelines, then secure any exposed fabric edges.

blocks.heading: Building decks and patios across sites?
blocks.body: DOTDAY supplies SHIELD and XBAR to contractors direct, by the roll or in bulk, matched to each ground condition.

blocks.heading: Get the weight right for the load
blocks.body: The most common mistake is using a light fabric under pavers. Under a compacted base and foot traffic it tears and lets aggregate sink, so paver areas need [XBAR](https://www.thedotday.com/product-page/dotday-xbar-weed-barrier-fabric-heavy-duty-5oz-dual-layer) 5oz dual-layer. Decks and shed floors put no real load on the fabric, so [SHIELD](https://www.thedotday.com/product-page/dotday-shield-weed-barrier-fabric-heavy-duty-3-2oz-landscape-fabric) 3.2oz is the cost-effective choice there for weed control and airflow. For a drainage trench beside the structure, [TERRA](https://www.thedotday.com/product-page/dotday-terra-geotextile-fabric-4-oz-non-woven-landscape-fabric) non-woven geotextile filters and flows water. For pavers specifically, see our guide on [weed barrier under pavers](https://www.thedotday.com/post/weed-barrier-under-pavers).

blocks.heading: Keep edges covered and overlapped
blocks.body: Two details decide longevity: overlap every seam at least 6 inches so weeds cannot break through, and never leave fabric exposed to sun at the edges, where UV makes it brittle within a couple of seasons. Tuck or anchor exposed edges under your border or gravel. To compare how long each weight lasts, read [what landscape fabric lasts the longest](https://www.thedotday.com/post/what-landscape-fabric-lasts-the-longest).

blocks.heading: Want the right fabric for your build?
blocks.body: Tell the DOTDAY team what you are building, deck, pavers, or shed, and the ground condition, and we will recommend the right fabric and quantity.

items.q: What fabric should go under a paver patio?
items.a: Use a 5oz dual-layer woven fabric like XBAR under pavers. The compacted aggregate base and foot traffic put real load on the fabric, and a lightweight barrier tears and lets the base sink into the soil. XBAR separates the base from the subsoil and resists puncture, placed beneath the compacted aggregate rather than directly under the sand setting bed.

items.q: Do you need fabric under a deck?
items.a: It helps. Under a raised deck, a woven weed barrier like SHIELD 3.2oz blocks weeds from growing up through the gravel or ground beneath, which is hard to reach once the deck is built. There is no structural load on the fabric there, so a 3.2oz woven is enough. Lay it over a cleared, leveled base and anchor it before the deck goes up.

items.q: What goes under a shed floor for weed control?
items.a: A 3.2oz woven weed barrier like SHIELD laid over a cleared, leveled base controls weeds under a shed without bearing load. Overlap seams at least 6 inches and anchor the fabric so it does not shift, then add your gravel pad or follow the shed's foundation guidelines on top. For heavier gravel pads with vehicle access, step up to XBAR.
